raw_data:
b' Money Stuff
Man, whenever Wharton puts Meme Financial Markets into its MBA core curriculum and hires me to teach it, this is going to be the final exam:

Man leads forward in chair

Man, whenever Wharton puts Meme Financial Markets into its MBA core curriculum and hires me to teach it, this is going to be the final exam :

GameStop Corp. shares surged as speculation swirled around a return to social media by Keith Gill, who drove the meme-stock mania of 2021 under the moniker \xe2\x80\x9cRoaring Kitty.\xe2\x80\x9d

The account\xe2\x80\x99s post on X showed a man leaning forward with what looked like a gaming controller, which some traders interpreted to mean that Gill is coming back into action. Gill, whose account has long been dormant, shot to fame in 2021 by rallying day traders on Reddit in an effort to squeeze GameStop short sellers.

The post had attracted more than 12 million views in the hours since its publication.\xc2\xa0Gill posted on X again at 11 a.m. New York time, this time a short video clip stating \xe2\x80\x9cFine, I\xe2\x80\x99ll do it myself.\xe2\x80\x9d

The reaction to Gill\xe2\x80\x99s post shows shades of the original 2021 mania, with chatter across Reddit\xe2\x80\x99s WallStreetBets and activity on StockTwits sparking a rush of retail trader buying.

\xe2\x80\x9cThat he is able to generate a crowd says that the crowd is back to feeling FOMO and YOLO in an enormous way,\xe2\x80\x9d said Peter Atwater, president of Financial Insyghts and an adjunct professor at William & Mary and the University of Delaware. \xe2\x80\x9cWhen people dive into things that are of pure speculative value, their confidence is extremely high and this is one of the ways that it manifests.\xe2\x80\x9d

Here is the first tweet , which does not make any fundamental or technical case\xc2\xa0for buying GameStop\xe2\x80\x99s stock, or make any other arguments for the stock, or mention GameStop, or contain any words at all. Just a drawing of the guy leaning forward. There are some ambiguous\xc2\xa0follow-up video clips . Prior to this, Gill had not tweeted since June 2021, and his YouTube channel has been similarly dormant. GameStop\xe2\x80\x99s\xc2\xa0stock opened this morning at $26.34, up 51% from Friday\xe2\x80\x99s close; it got as high as $38.20 at 10:05 a.m.,\xc2\xa0and was halted for volatility nine times in the first two hours of trading.\xc2\xa0Some\xc2\xa0exam questions:

  1. What do you think\xc2\xa0this tweet means?\xc2\xa0Is it about Gill\xe2\x80\x99s return to YouTube? To active stock trading? To GameStop? To some other stock? Is he getting into computer gaming\xc2\xa0content? Taking a\xc2\xa0life drawing class and tweeting his work?
  2. Let\xe2\x80\x99s say that you assign a\xc2\xa0probability of X to \xe2\x80\x9cthis tweet means that Gill is going to be back on YouTube talking about GameStop a lot\xe2\x80\x9d: How much does that change your GameStop price target? Build an Excel model.
  3. What probability would you assign to \xe2\x80\x9che\xe2\x80\x99s gonna be on YouTube talking about GameStop a lot, but now he\xe2\x80\x99s short\xe2\x80\x9d? How funny would that be?
  4. Is GameStop in a better or worse economic position than it was in January 2021, when Gill first sent it to the moon? Is the range of possible outcomes now wider or narrower?\xc2\xa0Has the pivot to online commerce paid off? Is the pivot to letting Chief Executive Officer Ryan Cohen trade stocks with GameStop\xe2\x80\x99s money going to pay off? The pivot to trading Pok\xc3\xa9mons ? Do you care about any of this at all? Is it rational to care about any of it? If you have a strongly held\xc2\xa0view of the company\xe2\x80\x99s fundamentals, what should you do about it?
  5. How much money\xc2\xa0has Citadel Securities made today because of this tweet?
  6. The article goes on to quote Vanda Research\xe2\x80\x99s Giacomo Pierantoni saying: \xe2\x80\x9cThese surges in retail activity have served as contrarian signals, prompting institutional investors to quickly short the stock following these rallies driven by retail investors.\xe2\x80\x9d Shorting GameStop? Really? Have the short sellers improved their timing since 2021, so that now they can short the stock following the rallies, rather than shorting early in the rallies and getting smoked? Or are the retail investors no longer as zealous and coordinated about punishing short sellers? Or were they not as zealous and coordinated about punishing short sellers, but now Roaring Kitty is back to lead them into battle?
  7. Consider this hypothetical\xc2\xa0trade: (1)\xc2\xa0Keith Gill buys a bunch of short-dated GameStop call options, (2) he tweets a picture of a guy leaning forward in a chair, (3) the stock shoots up, (4) he sells the options for a profit, (5) he never returns to YouTube or otherwise says anything about GameStop at all. Is this a good trade? How many times could he do this? If you bought GameStop on the basis of his tweet, and this turned out to be the trade, how mad would you be?
  8. If you bought GameStop on the basis of his tweet, and he turned out to be just tweeting from his drawing class, how mad would you be?
  9. In the hypothetical trade in Question 7, would the US Securities and Exchange Commission have a case against him for market manipulation? [1] In your answer, make reference to (1) the fact that the tweet doesn\xe2\x80\x99t mention GameStop at all, (2) the recent decision by a federal judge in Texas that actually pump and dumps are legal and (3) the 2023 decision by a federal judge in Washington, DC, that the moon emoji is securities fraud .
  10. Keith Gill seems to have added like $3 billion of market capitalization to GameStop by tweeting a picture of a guy. I assume he captured at\xc2\xa0most a tiny fraction of this value. Given this very particular skill that he has \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0the ability to add billions of dollars of value to, at least, this stock, with essentially no effort \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0what is the best way for him to monetize it? What is the highest-value use that financial markets could find for\xc2\xa0Keith Gill?
  11. If you were Ryan Cohen, would you hire Gill to run GameStop\xe2\x80\x99s investor relations? If you were, like, Tesla, would you hire Gill to run investor relations? Trump Media & Technology Group? Apple? How much would you pay him?
  12. If you were Keith Gill, would you go activist and try to take over GameStop? AMC Entertainment Holdings Inc.? Trump Media? Tesla? Apple?
  13. If you were Keith Gill, would you set up a hedge fund with a strategy like \xe2\x80\x9cI will opportunistically buy GameStop stock and then tweet to make it go up\xe2\x80\x9d? What percentage of your gains would you charge as a performance fee? How much money could you raise? From whom? How much could you deploy?\xc2\xa0(In your answer, consider Section 16 and GameStop\xe2\x80\x99s market capitalization.) Would you add capacity by tweeting about other companies? Which ones? Would your investors go for that?\xc2\xa0If you were invited to speak at Sohn , would you wear a bandana and pitch GameStop? Would you go out to dinner afterwards with Gabe Plotkin?
  14. If you ran a multistrategy hedge fund, would you hire Keith Gill to run a pod with a strategy like \xe2\x80\x9ctweet about GameStop and make it go up\xe2\x80\x9d? What kind of guarantee would you give him? How much capital would you allocate to him? How often would you want him to run this trade? Would he have to be factor-neutral? What would he do the rest of the time? Would you bring him to investor-relations\xc2\xa0meetings with limited partners?
  15. Is this just how life is now? In January 2021, the whole GameStop experience was new, and there was perhaps some genuine uncertainty about how things would turn out. (Could GameStop keep going up forever? Would there be the mother of all short squeezes? Could retail options buying create a perpetual motion machine?) And then the stock mostly fell back to earth, and the meme-stock machine ran again on other stocks with mostly diminishing results, and you could imagine that the whole thing was a confluence of novelty and social media and\xc2\xa0pandemic-era boredom and incautious short sellers that would not repeat itself. But in fact is this just a permanent feature of financial markets?

Here is Wharton\xe2\x80\x99s list of MBA finance classes , and there\xe2\x80\x99s not even an elective on meme stocks. There is FNCE7390, though, Behavioral Finance: \xe2\x80\x9cThere is an abundance of evidence suggesting that the standard economic paradigm \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0rational agents in an efficient market \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0does not adequately describe behavior in financial markets.\xe2\x80\x9d Yes.

RIP Jim Simons

James Simons, arguably the greatest of all hedge fund managers,\xc2\xa0died last week. Every obituary of Simons mentions the key facts of his career, which are that he knew (1) a lot about math and (2) nothing about finance. [2] This seems to have been a very fruitful combination. If you can program computers to analyze data with, as it were, an open mind, they will pick out signals from the data that work, and then you can trade on those signals and make an enormous fortune. If you insist on the signals making sense to you , you will just get in the way. Gregory Zuckerman writes :

Early on, Simons traded like most others, reading the news and betting on gold prices and other investments. He scored gains but found it hard to endure the market\xe2\x80\x99s volatility. He developed stomach pains and berated himself for miscues.

\xe2\x80\x9cOne day you\xe2\x80\x99re a genius, the next you feel like a dope,\xe2\x80\x9d Simons said. \xe2\x80\x9cFundamental trading gave me ulcers.\xe2\x80\x9d

Simons set out to build an automated trading system that could discover fleeting and overlooked patterns in prices.

\xe2\x80\x9cI want models that will make money while I sleep,\xe2\x80\x9d Simons told a friend at the time. \xe2\x80\x9cA pure system without humans interfering.\xe2\x80\x9d

So he made them, or hired people to. In 2010, Sebastian Mallaby summarized the approach of Simons\xe2\x80\x99s then-deputies Peter Brown and Robert Mercer [3] :

It\xe2\x80\x99s clear that the way Brown and Mercer approached programming was fundamentally different from the way other hedge-fund programmers thought about it. At Tudor, for example, Sushil Wadhwani trained a machine to approach markets in a way that made sense for human traders. By contrast, Brown and Mercer trained themselves to approach problems in a manner that made sense for computers. \xe2\x80\xa6\xc2\xa0Presented with apparently random data and no further clues, they sift it repeatedly for patterns, exploiting the power of computers to hunt for ghosts that to the human eye would be invisible. \xe2\x80\xa6

Robert Mercer \xe2\x80\xa6 [says]: \xe2\x80\x9cIf somebody came up with a theory about how the phases of Venus influence markets, we would want a lot of evidence.\xe2\x80\x9d But he adds that \xe2\x80\x9csome signals that make no intuitive sense do indeed work.\xe2\x80\x9d Indeed, it is the nonintuitive signals that often prove the most lucrative for Renaissance. \xe2\x80\x9cThe signals that we have been trading without interruption for fifteen years make no sense,\xe2\x80\x9d Mercer explains. \xe2\x80\x9cOtherwise someone else would have found them.\xe2\x80\x9d

Arguably the point of these two sections is that\xc2\xa0if your view of financial markets makes too much sense then it is probably wrong.

UBS

The thing about investment bankers is that they spend all day talking to rich people about money \xe2\x80\x94 meeting with corporate chief executive officers and private equity investors to discuss mergers and financing \xe2\x80\x94 but they would be hideously embarrassed to conclude those meetings by saying \xe2\x80\x9coh and what about you, do you have enough life insurance, could we help you plan for retirement?\xe2\x80\x9d The business of providing investment banking advice is totally separate from\xc2\xa0the business of providing personal financial advice. Even though the investment banker\xe2\x80\x99s company also probably has a huge department that provides personal financial advice and wealth management for rich people. And makes a lot of reliable recurring revenue doing it. And is looking to expand it.

And so every big investment bank will from time to time announce an initiative that is like \xe2\x80\x9cwe\xe2\x80\x99re going to tell our investment bankers that they should really mention our wealth management business to their corporate-executive clients,\xe2\x80\x9d and it really does tell the investment bankers that, and the investment bankers are like \xe2\x80\x9cyes we are team players and want to help our wealth management colleagues bring in more revenue,\xe2\x80\x9d but they shudder a little as they say it, and do they actually go out and pitch the wealth management business? I don\xe2\x80\x99t know, I\xe2\x80\x99m not in the meetings.

One obvious problem with this approach is that the wealth managers get paid for doing wealth management business, and the investment bankers get paid for doing investment banking business, and you can tell them to cross-sell because they are all team players, but are they? Whereas if you just give them a referral fee :

UBS Group AG is considering introducing a reward system for investment bankers who refer clients to the firm\xe2\x80\x99s wealth management business, according to people with knowledge of the matter.

Some bankers would likely be in line for payouts in Asia and Europe when they successfully attract new money to the lender\xe2\x80\x99s private banking unit, said the people, who asked not to be identified discussing confidential plans. The referral fees would be a first for the Zurich-based lender, echoing earlier incentives that Credit Suisse had offered to its dealmakers, the people said.

Part of me is like \xe2\x80\x9cyes obviously.\xe2\x80\x9d Part of me is like \xe2\x80\x9cno, being a team player is priceless, and giving people explicit bonuses for referrals cheapens it and makes people more mercenary and less cooperative.\xe2\x80\x9d But probably they are mercenary anyway and you might as well try the bonuses.

Outsider trading

If you work in a finance role at a public company, and you have early access to its earnings information, and you\xe2\x80\x99ve got an annoying friend who sometimes texts you to be like \xe2\x80\x9chey how\xe2\x80\x99s your company doing,\xe2\x80\x9d how should you respond? This is, I want to be clear, extra super not legal advice, but I feel like a reasonable response might be \xe2\x80\x9cwe are doing very well, thanks for asking, but\xc2\xa0if you are asking because you plan to trade our stock, please do not do that.\xe2\x80\x9d The essential elements of this response are:

  1. You do not give your friend any inside information. You do not give any details about performance, positive or negative.
  2. Oh, you do give a generic sunny statement that things are going \xe2\x80\x9cvery well.\xe2\x80\x9d You do this, to be clear, regardless of how things are going; if you know you are filing for bankruptcy tomorrow you still tell this guy \xe2\x80\x9cdoing very well.\xe2\x80\x9d It\xe2\x80\x99s none of his business! And \xe2\x80\x9cwe are doing very well\xe2\x80\x9d conveys no information \xe2\x80\x94 it\xe2\x80\x99s what any employee would generically say without any inside information \xe2\x80\x94 whereas \xe2\x80\x9cwe are not doing so great\xe2\x80\x9d does carry a bearish signal. Even \xe2\x80\x9cwe are doing okay\xe2\x80\x9d feels just a touch bearish, coming from a finance employee, no? \xe2\x80\x9cVery well\xe2\x80\x9d just seems like the neutral, information-free, politely loyal response.
  3. You tell your friend not to trade based on your conversations, because (1) if he trades and loses money, he will blame you, and you are not trying to convey any information at all, and (2) if he trades and makes money, after texting you, he\xe2\x80\x99ll get arrested and you will be in trouble for conveying the information that you did not intend to convey.

Again, I must emphasize that this is not legal advice, or even right apparently, because here\xe2\x80\x99s a pretty wild US Securities and Exchange Commission case :

The Securities and Exchange Commission [Friday] charged Gibsonia, PA resident Frank T. Poerio, Jr. with insider trading for misappropriating material, nonpublic information concerning Dick\xe2\x80\x99s Sporting Goods Inc. and trading on that information, realizing illegal profits of more than $800,000.

According to the SEC\xe2\x80\x99s complaint, between November 2019 and May 2021, Poerio knew a Dick\xe2\x80\x99s employee who had access to multiple internal sources of material nonpublic information about the company\xe2\x80\x99s financial results. The SEC alleges that Poerio frequently asked the employee for updates on the company\xe2\x80\x99s performance, and, at times, the employee responded with statements to the effect that the company was \xe2\x80\x9cdoing very well,\xe2\x80\x9d coupled with requests that Poerio not trade in Dick\xe2\x80\x99s securities. The SEC alleges that Poerio understood that these statements were based on the employee\xe2\x80\x99s access to nonpublic financial information and previews of the company\xe2\x80\x99s upcoming quarterly revenue figures. Poerio used this information to trade in Dick\xe2\x80\x99s securities, in breach of his duty of trust and confidence to the employee.

\xe2\x80\x9cWe allege that Poerio exploited the trust of someone close to him by pumping that person for information and then ignoring his repeated requests not to trade on that information,\xe2\x80\x9d said Mark Cave, Associate Director of the SEC\xe2\x80\x99s Division of Enforcement. \xe2\x80\x9cToday\xe2\x80\x99s charges showcase the SEC\xe2\x80\x99s ability to detect and charge those who seek to profit off insider trading.\xe2\x80\x9d

Here is the complaint, which contains more details on the insider\xe2\x80\x99s access to information (including a real-time database with store-level sales information) and Poerio\xe2\x80\x99s trading (he bought stock and call options, and sold put options, around earnings releases, and made profits of $823,367), but no more details on their conversations. Just, like:

During the time of Individual A\xe2\x80\x99s employment with Dick\xe2\x80\x99s Sporting Goods, Poerio frequently asked Individual A for updates on the company\xe2\x80\x99s performance, despite knowing that insider trading was illegal. On some occasions, Individual A responded by telling Poerio that such information could not be disclosed.

On other occasions, however, Individual A made statements to Poerio to the effect that the company was \xe2\x80\x9cdoing very well,\xe2\x80\x9d and then reiterated requests that Poerio not trade in the securities of Dick\xe2\x80\x99s Sporting Goods. Poerio understood that these statements about the company\xe2\x80\x99s performance were based on Individual A\xe2\x80\x99s access to nonpublic revenue numbers, and that they provided a preview of the company\xe2\x80\x99s upcoming quarterly financial reports.

That is a lot to understand from the words \xe2\x80\x9cdoing very well\xe2\x80\x9d! I think this is the worst insider tip I\xe2\x80\x99ve ever seen. To be fair, I am only reading the words; there are ways to pronounce the words \xe2\x80\x9cdoing very well\xe2\x80\x9d that mean \xe2\x80\x9cbuy as much as you can\xe2\x80\x9d rather than \xe2\x80\x9cfine thanks for asking.\xe2\x80\x9d Also, the SEC might be holding something back. For instance, in advance of Dick\xe2\x80\x99s November 2020 earnings, \xe2\x80\x9cPoerio and Individual A spoke on the phone at least 122 times, a number which does not include texts or in-person meetings.\xe2\x80\x9d Possibly they got into more detail there than \xe2\x80\x9cdoing very well.\xe2\x80\x9d [4]

Everything is securities fraud

I think sometimes about starting a spinoff Everything Is Securities Fraud newsletter that is just a weekly list of cases, with links but no other content. There\xe2\x80\x99s not much to say about them at this point, but they do keep happening. Two big ones from last week:

  • \xe2\x80\x9cBoeing\xe2\x80\x99s Safety Claims Probed by SEC After Panel Blew Off Plane .\xe2\x80\x9d
  • \xe2\x80\x9cIn Tesla Autopilot probe , US prosecutors focus on securities, wire fraud.\xe2\x80\x9d

If someone is killed in a car crash, what is that? Why that\xe2\x80\x99s securities fraud. If a door flies off a plane in mid-air, what\xe2\x80\x99s that? That, too, is securities fraud. Creating substantive regulations and deterring misconduct in the real world is messy and complicated, but in the abstract world of finance, everything is securities fraud.

Podcast

The latest Money Stuff podcast features\xc2\xa0Katie Greifeld and me talking about the FTX bankruptcy, private markets, single-stock exchange-traded funds and, for some reason, the safest way to enter a pool of pudding. You can listen to it here .

Pay to play

We talked a couple of times last week about situations in which you can pay to play professional sports. The main ones I mentioned were Portuguese soccer (one time), car racing (often) and bridge (pretty much all the time). Afterwards a bunch of readers emailed to mention polo , which honestly seems obvious in hindsight.

Things happen

BHP Says Anglo American Has Rejected Revised Proposal . Consulting firms\xe2\x80\x99 grip on Saudi economy sparks local misgivings. Squarespace to Go Private in $6.6 Billion Takeover by Permira. China reduces access to live data on share trades by foreign investors. The Long, Steep Fall of an Online Education Giant. What\xe2\x80\x99s So Bad About Fixing Oil Prices ? \xe2\x80\x9cThis is the only possibility for a buyer to get a position in the High Arctic and establish a strategic foothold .\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cDart throws, if done right , really can beat the market in the long run.\xe2\x80\x9d Chicken Wings Are the New Inflation Hedge . Farewell, Chuck E. Cheese Animatronic Band .

If you'd like to get\xc2\xa0Money\xc2\xa0Stuff\xc2\xa0in handy email form, right in your inbox, please subscribe at this link . Or you can subscribe to Money Stuff and other great Bloomberg newsletters here . Thanks!

[1] One reader suggested *insider trading*? I think the answer there is obviously no but I guess it goes on the exam.

[2] He also knew a lot about *recruiting* mathematicians, and had the important intuition that he should keep doing that when he left academia for finance, and not recruit finance people.

[3] This is from pages 301 and 302 of More Money Than God , and yes, there is a discussion of the risks of data mining that I have omitted (but that Renaissance obviously understands).

[4] Except that if the insider was giving him exhaustive financial updates, why wasn\xe2\x80\x99t *he* charged? The SEC\xe2\x80\x99s theory here is that Individual A was innocently talking to Poerio and expecting him not to trade, which works for \xe2\x80\x9cdoing very well\xe2\x80\x9d but not for, like, 122 detailed due diligence sessions.
